do.narrate: The plaza at the edge of town: six storefronts, four occupied, one of them 'Kim's Nails,' its window glowing warm against the gray afternoon. And there, around the side, exactly where Merle filed it — a gray steel door with no sign, no handle-cam, no branding. Just a hum. You can feel it through your shoes: fifty hertz of institutional indifference, the actual edge of the actual internet, breathing behind a nail salon.

You stand there in your Real Pants, coat over your arm, mug in your bag, four wrong names deep into the first day of the rest of the outage. Every dashboard on Earth is red, and the only trail on Earth runs through that door. Mel Function — whatever this town thinks your name is — the descent starts here.
END OF ACT ONE. (Act Two: 'Regional Infrastructure' — under construction. The hum will wait. It's good at waiting.)

onEnter.text: The plaza, the salon, the hum. Act Two is behind that door, and it isn't finished being built. The door, notably, doesn't care.

look.text: The plaza's pylon sign: PARKVIEW PLAZA in letters missing enough plastic to read PARKV EW P AZA. Below, tenant panels — the nail salon's is lit. One panel is blank white and always has been. That one, you now know, is the internet's.
Weeds working a seam in the parking lot, patient as rust. Zero funding, zero roadmap, one hundred percent year-over-year growth. The only scaling story in this town that ever came true.

Kim's Nails, open and busy — a generator drones out back, because Kim, alone among the businesses of this county, had a continuity plan. Through the window, a manicure proceeds in defiance of the apocalypse. The internet's last neighbor is thriving.

open.text: Not yet. Kim keeps information the way the diner keeps pie: dispensed on her schedule, to the deserving. Act Two problem.

talk.text: Through the glass, Kim points you — without looking up from a cuticle — around the side, toward the hum. Everyone who comes here in a hurry wants the gray door. Kim has seen your kind before.

look.text: Above the gray door, where a transom window should be: a consumer box fan, wedged and duct-taped, exhausting warm air at a steady clip. Somewhere a datacenter engineer has a nightmare that looks exactly like this. This is the cooling architecture. This is 'regional.'

A gray steel door, sun-faded, unlabeled except for a sticker the size of a playing card: a stylized cloud and the words 'CUMULONIMBUS EDGE — AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL.' Someone has ballpointed 'nimbus' onto it; corporate stickered over the graffiti; the graffiti returned. Behind the door, the hum holds a perfect, patient note. The whole county's internet lives in there, and it is not feeling well.

open.text: Locked — a real lock, a metal one, the janitor's-key kind. Getting through this door is the entire shape of Act Two, and Act Two is still being wired. The hum approves of your ambition and declines your entry.

use.text: You rest a palm on the steel. Warm. Vibrating. Alive, in the way that sick things are still alive. 'Soon,' you tell it, and mean it professionally.

talk.text: You and the door listen to each other hum. It's the most productive status meeting you've attended all year.

look.text: The plaza dumpster. Poking from the top: a shipping box for a 48-port switch, a coil of cable offcuts, and an empty energy-drink flat. Archaeology confirms the closet has been maintained exactly once, by a young man, in a hurry, some years ago.

open.text: You are not yet at the dumpster-diving stage of this incident. That's Act Two, possibly Act Three. Pace the descent.

look.text: The Parkview Plaza, which views a parking lot. Tanning, tax prep, vacancy, nails, vacancy, and one unmarked unit. Regional infrastructure chose this place for the same reason everyone else here did: the rent.
